question: Explain Clean Architecture in Flutter with layers and responsibilities.
answer:
Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin) organizes code in concentric layers with the Dependency Rule: outer layers depend on inner layers, NEVER the reverse.

Three layers (inside out):

1. Domain (innermost, pure Dart):
   • Entities — core business objects (no framework dependencies)
   • Use Cases — single business operations
   • Repository interfaces — abstract contracts

2. Data (implements domain contracts):
   • Repository implementations
   • Remote data sources (API)
   • Local data sources (database/cache)
   • Data models (JSON-capable)

3. Presentation (outer):
   • Blocs/Cubits/ViewModels
   • Pages and Widgets
   • Only knows domain objects through use cases

Testability: domain is tested with zero dependencies; data with mocked sources; presentation with mocked use cases.

question: How do you structure a large Flutter app by features (feature-first)?
answer:
Feature-first (vertical slicing) groups files by FEATURE rather than by layer.

Layer-first (horizontal):
lib/models/ lib/repositories/ lib/blocs/ lib/screens/
Problem: Adding a feature requires touching 4+ folders.

Feature-first (vertical):
lib/features/auth/ lib/features/cart/ lib/features/profile/
Each feature folder contains its own models, bloc, repo, screens, widgets.

Benefits:
• High cohesion — related code sits together
• Easy to delete/add features
• Teams can own features independently
• Clear boundaries → better for micro-frontends / modularization

Convention: shared code lives in lib/core/ or lib/shared/

question: Compare MVC, MVP, MVVM, and MVI patterns and how they map to Flutter.
answer:
All four organize the relationship between UI and business logic but differ in who calls whom and how data flows.

MVC (Model-View-Controller):
• Controller mediates between Model and View — V calls C, C updates M, M notifies V
• Most general, often vague in modern apps; modern "MVC" frameworks usually mean MVP or MVVM

MVP (Model-View-Presenter):
• Presenter holds the logic; View is dumb and only renders / dispatches user actions
• View has an interface (`MyView { showLoading(); showUser(User u); }`) the Presenter calls
• Common in Android Java; in Flutter it has been mostly displaced by MVVM/Bloc

MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel):
• ViewModel exposes observable state (`Stream<State>` / `ChangeNotifier`)
• View binds to that state and rebuilds reactively — no manual presenter calls
• Closest fit for Flutter: Riverpod's Notifier, Provider's ChangeNotifier, BLoC/Cubit, GetX, MobX
• `ConsumerWidget`/`BlocBuilder`/`Observer` are the binding

MVI (Model-View-Intent):
• Single state object per screen, unidirectional data flow: Intent → reducer → State → View
• Inspired by Redux/Elm; in Flutter it's BLoC's standard event/state model — name aside, you're already there

In Flutter today: "MVVM" or "BLoC pattern" are the two terms you'll hear most. They both fit the framework's reactive nature; choose based on team experience and library preference.

question: What does it mean to apply SOLID principles in Flutter?
answer:
SOLID is a set of object-oriented design principles. Applied loosely in Flutter:

Single Responsibility — every class does one thing:
• A widget renders UI; not networking, not business logic
• A repository fetches data; not formatting it for display
• Tells you when to split: when describing the class needs the word "and"

Open/Closed — open for extension, closed for modification:
• Add new subclasses of a sealed family without rewriting consumers
• Use the Strategy pattern (PaymentStrategy interface) so a new payment processor doesn't change the checkout class

Liskov Substitution — subtypes should be substitutable for their base:
• If repo: UserRepository, swapping in MockUserRepository should not break callers
• Don't tighten preconditions or weaken postconditions in a subclass

Interface Segregation — many small interfaces beat one big one:
• Don't make consumers depend on methods they don't use
• Split `RepositoryFat` into `Reader`, `Writer`, `Cacher` — clients depend only on what they use

Dependency Inversion — depend on abstractions, not concretions:
• Bloc depends on `AuthRepository` interface, not on `DioAuthRepositoryImpl`
• Inject the implementation via constructor (or DI framework)
• Lets you mock for tests and swap implementations per environment

Pragmatic notes:
• SOLID is a guide, not a religion. Over-applying creates fragmentation (every class is an interface, every method has its own file).
• Single Responsibility and Dependency Inversion are the two that pay off most consistently in Flutter apps.

question: How do you split a large Flutter app into modular packages or workspaces?
answer:
Once a Flutter codebase passes ~50k lines or 3+ teams, packing everything into one `lib/` becomes painful: long compile cycles, accidental cross-feature imports, no enforced boundaries. Splitting into local packages is the standard remedy.

Approaches:

1) Monorepo with local packages (Melos / Dart 3 workspaces):
• Each feature is its own `package:my_app_<feature>` in `packages/`
• A top-level `app/` package depends on them and ties everything together
• Use Melos to bootstrap, run scripts across packages, manage versions; or, in Dart 3.5+, use built-in workspaces (`pubspec.yaml` `workspace:`)
• Boundaries: package A can only import from package B if it explicitly depends on it — no accidental imports

2) Plugin packages for native code:
• Anything that ships its own Kotlin/Swift goes into a Flutter plugin package — even if it's only used by your app
• Cleaner Android/iOS module separation

3) Pure-Dart packages for shared logic:
• `domain/` (entities, use cases) and `core/` utilities are great candidates
• They have no Flutter dependency, so they can be tested fast and reused outside the app (e.g., a CLI or web)

What to split:
• Each independently-owned feature: auth, billing, search, etc.
• Cross-cutting: theming, networking client, logging
• Generated code: Pigeon / GraphQL / API stubs

What NOT to split:
• Tiny app — splitting adds ceremony for no gain
• Tight coupling between two "features" — fix the coupling first, then split

Gotcha: package boundaries push you to make APIs explicit. That's the point — but expect a refactor as you formalize public surfaces.
code_example:
# ── Dart 3.5+ workspaces (recommended for new monorepos) ──
# Top-level pubspec.yaml
name: my_app_workspace
environment: { sdk: ^3.5.0 }
workspace:
  - app
  - packages/auth
  - packages/billing
  - packages/core
  - packages/domain
  - packages/networking

# Each package has its own pubspec, but `flutter pub get` from the root resolves them together.
# Cross-package deps are real version-pinned dependencies; the analyzer enforces them.

# packages/auth/pubspec.yaml
name: my_app_auth
resolution: workspace
dependencies:
  flutter: { sdk: flutter }
  my_app_domain: any        # pure-Dart domain layer
  my_app_networking: any

# app/pubspec.yaml
name: my_app
resolution: workspace
dependencies:
  flutter: { sdk: flutter }
  my_app_auth:    any
  my_app_billing: any
  my_app_core:    any

# ── Or: Melos (still common, supports older SDKs) ──
# melos.yaml
name: my_app
packages:
  - app
  - packages/**
scripts:
  analyze:
    run: flutter analyze
    exec:
      concurrency: 4
  test:
    run: flutter test
    exec:
      concurrency: 2

# Bootstrap (once, after clone)
# dart pub global activate melos
# melos bootstrap
# melos run analyze
# melos run test

# ── Module structure ──
# packages/auth/lib/
#   auth.dart                    ← public surface (barrel file)
#   src/                         ← internal code, NOT exported
#     auth_repository.dart
#     auth_repository_impl.dart
#     auth_bloc.dart
#     pages/login_page.dart
#
# packages/auth/lib/auth.dart
export 'src/auth_repository.dart' show AuthRepository;
export 'src/auth_bloc.dart'         show AuthBloc, AuthState;
export 'src/pages/login_page.dart'  show LoginPage;
# Implementation classes stay private.

# ── App composition ──
# app/lib/main.dart
# import 'package:my_app_auth/auth.dart';
# import 'package:my_app_billing/billing.dart';
# void main() => runApp(MyApp());

question: How do you map between domain entities and data DTOs cleanly?
answer:
The data layer talks JSON; the domain layer talks Dart. Putting `fromJson`/`toJson` directly on your domain entities couples business logic to the wire format — a bad idea for anything beyond a tiny prototype.

Clean separation:

• Domain entity (`User`) — pure Dart. Constructor, fields, equality, no JSON.
• Data DTO (`UserDto`) — knows how to (de)serialize. Lives in the data layer.
• Mapper — converts DTO ↔ entity. Either an extension method, a top-level function, or a static method on the DTO.

Why this pays off:
• You can refactor the JSON shape without breaking domain or UI
• You can drop fields that aren't business-relevant (debug timestamps, internal IDs)
• You can add fields the wire doesn't carry (computed, defaulted, or denormalized from another DTO)
• You can have multiple DTOs (REST + GraphQL + cached) all mapping to the same entity

When to skip:
• Throwaway prototype, tiny single-feature app — the DTO/entity overhead may not pay
• When using freezed throughout and the API exactly matches your domain shape — though you'll regret it the first time the API changes

Gotcha: do NOT inherit DTO from entity to save typing — that breaks the layer rule (DTOs should not leak into domain code). Use composition or a mapper.

question: How do you design a typed AppError hierarchy that flows from data layer to UI?
answer:
Every layer in a Flutter app can fail differently. If you let exceptions bubble up untyped, your UI ends up with `if (e.toString().contains('401'))` — fragile and unmaintainable. A small sealed AppError tree, mapped at each layer boundary, gives you UI-friendly errors with no leaks.

Design:

1) Domain defines `sealed class AppError` with subtypes that the UI knows how to render:
   - NetworkError, TimeoutError, ServerError(int), UnauthorizedError, ForbiddenError, NotFoundError, ValidationError(fields), CancelledError, UnknownError

2) Data layer maps low-level exceptions (DioException, SocketException, FormatException) into AppErrors. The repository's signature throws AppError, never DioException.

3) Domain use cases just rethrow — they're already typed.

4) Presentation uses a sealed switch to decide what to do per error:
   - UnauthorizedError → redirect to login
   - ValidationError → highlight fields
   - NetworkError → show offline banner with retry
   - default → generic toast

Why sealed:
• Pattern matching is exhaustive — adding a new error class fails every switch in the codebase until handled
• No untyped string parsing
• Encourages thinking about user-visible categories instead of HTTP codes

Don't model business outcomes as errors:
• If "username already taken" is a normal flow, return a Result<Ok|Conflict>, don't throw. Reserve errors for unexpected, exceptional cases.
• Validation errors are an edge case — they're often modeled as both. Pick one and stay consistent.

question: How do feature flags and staged rollouts fit into a Flutter app's architecture?
answer:
Feature flags decouple deploy from release: ship the code dark, flip a switch later for some or all users. Done right, they're a multiplier — risky changes ship safely, A/B tests are cheap, rollbacks are instant.

Layers:

1) Flag store — where the truth lives:
• Remote config (Firebase Remote Config, GrowthBook, LaunchDarkly, ConfigCat) — server-side, can target by user/cohort/version
• Bundled defaults — a JSON shipped with the app so the first launch (no network) has reasonable behavior
• Local overrides — hidden settings screen for QA to flip flags without server access

2) Flag client — a thin Dart abstraction:
• `FeatureFlags.checkoutV2Enabled` returns bool
• Caches the latest fetched values in memory + secure storage so a transient network hiccup doesn't toggle features
• Refreshes on app foreground and after auth changes

3) Branching points — where flags are read:
• At the composition root: pick which Notifier/Bloc to instantiate
• In feature modules: hide menu items, choose code path
• Use sparingly inside hot paths — flag lookups are cheap but if-soup ages badly

4) Cleanup discipline:
• Every flag has a lifetime: experiment → ramp → remove
• Tag the code (`// FLAG: checkout_v2 — remove after 2026-06-01`)
• Schedule a cleanup task (this app's `/schedule` skill is exactly for this — open a removal PR after the ramp)

Gotchas:
• Don't gate critical security checks behind a flag — bypass risk
• Make sure flag state is part of analytics events so you can correlate metrics to variant
• Test BOTH branches in CI; otherwise the disabled branch quietly bit-rots until you flip and discover it's broken

question: How do you set up dependency injection at scale (composition root, scopes, environments)?
answer:
Dependency injection in a real app needs three things beyond "wire constructors in main()":

1) A composition root
• A single place where the dependency graph is constructed
• In Flutter that's `main.dart` or an `inject.dart` initializer
• Avoid resolving dependencies inside business code; resolve at the root and pass constructor args from there

2) Scopes
• App scope — singletons living for the lifetime of the app: Dio, AuthRepository, Logger
• User scope — recreated when a different user signs in: PreferencesNotifier, UserSpecificCache
• Screen / feature scope — owned by a route or a state holder; auto-disposed when no longer used
• Riverpod's `.autoDispose` and family modifier model these naturally; get_it has manual scopes via `pushNewScope`

3) Environments / flavors
• Override providers per build flavor (dev / staging / prod) — e.g., a fake API in dev, real one in prod
• Runtime overrides for QA/feature flags ("force the slow-net implementation to test loading states")
• Tests are just another environment — override the same providers with fakes

Guidelines:
• Bind interfaces, not concrete types — your bloc shouldn't see `DioAuthRepositoryImpl`, only `AuthRepository`
• Don't eagerly instantiate singletons that aren't always needed; both Riverpod and get_it support lazy by default
• Document the graph: a one-page README listing what's app-scoped, user-scoped, screen-scoped saves new joiners hours
• Avoid the service-locator anti-pattern of `MyService.instance.method()` baked into business code — it's just a global with extra steps
